List the paper filename and a brief summary.\n\nPapers to read:\n- PMC4618180.md\n- PMC4689517.md\n- PMC4765184.md\n- PMC4810765.md\n- PMC4859410.md\n- PMC4892373.md\n- PMC4892378.md\n- PMC5301237.md\n- PMC5877743.md\n- PMC6863998.md\n- PMC8953705.md\n\nRun: for f in PMC4618180 PMC4689517 PMC4765184 PMC4810765 PMC4859410 PMC4892373 PMC4892378 PMC5301237 PMC5877743 PMC6863998 PMC8953705; do echo \"=== $f ===\"; cat /app/papers/$f.md; echo; done 2>&1 | head -4000","agentId":"ad95a8c","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I now have enough information to provide a comprehensive summary of all 11 papers. Here is the analysis:\n\n---\n\n## Summary of Papers and Relevance to rs12979860 (IL28B rs12979860)\n\n---\n\n### PMC4618180.md\n**Title:** Response to treatment following recently acquired hepatitis C virus infection in a multi-centre collaborative cohort\n\n**Relevant to rs12979860:** YES\n\n**Summary:** This multi-centre cohort study examined predictors of sustained virological response (SVR) in 237 patients with recently acquired HCV treated with pegylated interferon (PEG-IFN), with or without ribavirin. The IFNL4 genotype (determined by sequencing rs12979860 or rs8099917) was an independent predictor of SVR (aOR 2.27, p=0.021). The CC genotype (favorable allele at rs12979860) was associated with better treatment response. Other predictors included duration of infection, baseline HCV RNA level, and age.\n\n- **Drugs:** Pegylated interferon-alfa 2a or 2b (PEG-IFN), ribavirin (RBV)\n- **Phenotypes/Conditions:** Acute/recently acquired hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection, sustained virological response (SVR)\n\n---\n\n### PMC4689517.md\n**Title:** IFNL3 and IFNL4 genotypes in hepatitis C genotype 2/3 patients treated with PEG-IFN and ribavirin (German registry)\n\n**Relevant to rs12979860:** YES\n\n**Summary:** A large German prospective registry (1006 patients) examined IFNL3 rs12979860, rs8099917, and IFNL4 ss469415590 genotypes as predictors of rapid virological response (RVR) and SVR in HCV genotype 2/3 patients treated with PEG-IFN and ribavirin. rs12979860 was significantly associated with RVR (p<0.0001) but not with SVR (p=0.251) in this genotype 2/3 population. The study found 99.2% concordance between rs12979860 CC and IFNL4 TT/TT genotypes. IFNL4 genotyping offered no additional predictive value over IFNL3 in Caucasian GT2/3 patients.\n\n- **Drugs:** Pegylated interferon alfa-2b (PEG-IFN), ribavirin (RBV)\n- **Phenotypes/Conditions:** Chronic hepatitis C (HCV genotype 2 and 3), rapid virological response (RVR), sustained virological response (SVR)\n\n---\n\n### PMC4765184.md\n**Title:** Vitamin D-related and IL28B rs12979860 gene polymorphisms predict SVR in Egyptian chronic hepatitis C genotype 4 patients\n\n**Relevant to rs12979860:** YES\n\n**Summary:** A cohort study of 200 Egyptian patients with chronic hepatitis C genotype 4 (CHC G4) treated with pegylated interferon-alpha plus ribavirin. IL28B rs12979860 was the strongest single predictor of SVR (C/T+T/T genotypes inversely associated with SVR, OR=0.064, p<0.001). VDR rs2228570 and CYP2R1 rs10741657 polymorphisms were also independently associated with SVR. Combining IL28B CC genotype with VDR A/A genotype increased SVR prediction to 100%. The rs12979860 CC genotype alone predicted SVR in 80% of patients.\n\n- **Drugs:** Pegylated interferon-alpha (Peg-IFN), ribavirin (RBV)\n- **Phenotypes/Conditions:** Chronic hepatitis C (HCV genotype 4), sustained virological response (SVR), liver fibrosis\n\n---\n\n### PMC4810765.md\n**Title:** Sacubitril is selectively activated by carboxylesterase 1 (CES1) in the liver and the activation is affected by CES1 genetic variation\n\n**Relevant to rs12979860:** NO\n\n**Summary:** This pharmacology study investigated the enzymatic activation of sacubitril (a prodrug used for heart failure) by carboxylesterase 1 (CES1) in human liver. It found that sacubitril is a selective CES1 substrate, and the CES1 variant G143E (rs71647871) is a loss-of-function variant that significantly impairs sacubitril activation. No mention of rs12979860, IL28B, or hepatitis C.\n\n- **Drugs:** Sacubitril (prodrug for heart failure, activated to LBQ657), valsartan, enalapril\n- **Phenotypes/Conditions:** Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction; CES1 pharmacogenetics\n\n---\n\n### PMC4859410.md\n**Title:** HIV protease inhibitors are substrates for OATP1A2, OATP1B1 and OATP1B3, and lopinavir plasma concentrations are influenced by SLCO1B1 polymorphisms\n\n**Relevant to rs12979860:** NO\n\n**Summary:** This study examined the organic anion transporting polypeptides (OATPs) OATP1A2, OATP1B1, and OATP1B3 as transporters for HIV antiretroviral protease inhibitors. Using a Xenopus laevis oocyte expression system, it was shown that HIV protease inhibitors (saquinavir, lopinavir, darunavir) but not non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (nevirapine, efavirenz) are substrates for these OATPs. The SLCO1B1 521T>C (rs4149056) polymorphism was significantly associated with higher lopinavir plasma concentrations. No mention of rs12979860, IL28B, or hepatitis C.\n\n- **Drugs:** Lopinavir, saquinavir, darunavir, ritonavir, nevirapine, efavirenz (HIV antiretrovirals)\n- **Phenotypes/Conditions:** HIV infection, pharmacokinetics of protease inhibitors, SLCO1B1 pharmacogenetics\n\n---\n\n### PMC4892373.md\n**Title:** IL28B gene polymorphisms and HCV treatment response in HIV/HCV-coinfected patients (first study of rs12979860 in this population)\n\n**Relevant to rs12979860:** YES\n\n**Summary:** This is described as the first study to report a strong influence of rs12979860 near the IL28B gene on response to pegIFN-RBV in HIV/HCV-coinfected patients. Among 164 HIV/HCV-coinfected patients genotyped for rs12979860, the CC genotype was found in 46% of patients and was strongly associated with SVR (75% CC vs. 38% CT/TT, p<0.0001). In multivariable analysis, rs12979860 CC genotype was an independent predictor of SVR (OR 3.7, 95% CI 1.6-8.5, p=0.002). The effect was significant for HCV genotypes 1 and 4, but not genotype 3. The CC genotype frequency was also higher in patients with spontaneous HCV clearance (75% vs. 46% chronic).\n\n- **Drugs:** Peginterferon (PEG-IFN), ribavirin (RBV)\n- **Phenotypes/Conditions:** Chronic hepatitis C (HCV genotypes 1, 3, 4) in HIV/HCV-coinfected patients, sustained virological response (SVR), spontaneous HCV clearance\n\n---\n\n### PMC4892378.md\n**Title:** IL28B gene polymorphisms and viral kinetics in HIV/hepatitis C virus-coinfected patients treated with pegylated interferon and ribavirin\n\n**Relevant to rs12979860:** YES\n\n**Summary:** This study of 196 HIV/HCV-coinfected patients examined the influence of IL28B rs12979860 on on-treatment viral kinetics and virological responses (RVR, EVR, cEVR, end-of-treatment response, and SVR). The CC genotype (44% prevalence) was associated with significantly greater HCV RNA declines at weeks 4 and 12 of therapy and with higher rates of all virological response milestones. The CC genotype remained a predictor of SVR even in patients who did not achieve RVR (OR 8.7, p=0.001). Effects were confined to HCV genotypes 1 and 4, not genotypes 2/3.\n\n- **Drugs:** Pegylated interferon-alpha (PEG-IFN), ribavirin (RBV)\n- **Phenotypes/Conditions:** Chronic hepatitis C (HCV genotypes 1, 3, 4) in HIV/HCV-coinfected patients, viral kinetics, rapid virological response (RVR), early virological response (EVR), sustained virological response (SVR)\n\n---\n\n### PMC5301237.md\n**Title:** IFNL4 ss469415590 and IL28B rs12979860 predict HCV clearance in a Chinese population\n\n**Relevant to rs12979860:** YES\n\n**Summary:** This study of Chinese Han populations (795 chronic HCV carriers, 460 subjects with natural HCV clearance, and 362 patients treated with PEG-IFN/RBV) investigated both IFNL4 ss469415590 and IL28B rs12979860 as predictors of spontaneous and treatment-induced HCV clearance. Both SNPs independently predicted SVR in multivariate analysis; rs12979860 T variant was negatively associated with clearance (OR 0.59, p<0.001 for spontaneous; OR 0.34 in treatment group). Linkage disequilibrium between ss469415590 and rs12979860 was only moderate in this Chinese cohort (r2=0.62), unlike in Caucasians. Combined unfavorable alleles of both SNPs dramatically reduced clearance rates.\n\n- **Drugs:** Pegylated interferon-alpha (PEG-IFN), ribavirin (RBV)\n- **Phenotypes/Conditions:** Chronic hepatitis C (HCV genotype 1), spontaneous HCV clearance, sustained virological response (SVR), rapid virological response (RVR), early virological response (EVR)\n\n---\n\n### PMC5877743.md\n**Title:** Prediction of tacrolimus exposure by CYP3A5 genotype and exposure of co-administered everolimus in Japanese renal transplant recipients\n\n**Relevant to rs12979860:** NO\n\n**Summary:** This pharmacokinetic study evaluated how tacrolimus AUC (area under the blood concentration-time curve) can be predicted based on CYP3A5 genotype and co-administered everolimus AUC in Japanese renal transplant recipients. CYP3A5*3/*3 patients showed higher dose-adjusted AUC/D for tacrolimus and stronger correlations with everolimus AUC than CYP3A5*1 allele carriers. No mention of rs12979860, IL28B, or hepatitis C.\n\n- **Drugs:** Tacrolimus, everolimus (immunosuppressants)\n- **Phenotypes/Conditions:** Renal transplantation, CYP3A5 pharmacogenetics, drug pharmacokinetics\n\n---\n\n### PMC6863998.md\n**Title:** IFNL4 haplotype, linkage disequilibrium and their influence on virological response to hepatitis C virus infection in Indian population\n\n**Relevant to rs12979860:** YES\n\n**Summary:** This case-control and cohort study of north Indian CHC patients (n=141) and healthy controls (n=111) examined IFNL4 SNPs rs12979860 and rs8099917 with respect to HCV exposure risk and SVR to PEG-IFN/RBV therapy. In this population, the rs12979860 CC genotype and rs8099917 TT genotype, as well as the C-T haplotype, were significantly associated with SVR (p=0.009 and p=0.021). Linkage disequilibrium between rs12979860 and rs8099917 was very high in CHC cases (D'=0.98) but lower in healthy controls (D'=0.76). The minor allele (T) frequency at rs12979860 was 0.241 in CHC cases and 0.229 in healthy controls.\n\n- **Drugs:** Pegylated interferon (PEG-IFN), ribavirin (RBV)\n- **Phenotypes/Conditions:** Chronic hepatitis C (CHC), sustained virological response (SVR), HCV exposure, hepatocellular carcinoma risk (referenced), hepatic inflammation and fibrosis (referenced)\n\n---\n\n### PMC8953705.md\n**Title:** STOP Pain Project — Opioid response in pediatric cancer patients and gene polymorphisms of cytokine pathways\n\n**Relevant to rs12979860:** NO\n\n**Summary:** This prospective observational cohort study investigated the association between cytokine gene SNPs (IL-6 rs1800795, rs1800796, rs1800797; IL-8 rs4073; TNFa rs1800629) and response to opioid therapy in 74 pediatric cancer patients. IL-6 rs1800797 was associated with higher baseline pain intensity and higher opioid dose requirements. TNFa rs1800629 GG genotype was associated with higher total opioid doses. No mention of rs12979860, IL28B, or hepatitis C.\n\n- **Drugs:** Morphine, codeine, oxycodone, fentanyl, tramadol (opioid analgesics)\n- **Phenotypes/Conditions:** Pediatric cancer pain, opioid dose requirements, cytokine pharmacogenetics\n\n---\n\n## Summary Table\n\n| File | Relevant to rs12979860 | Drugs Discussed | Phenotypes/Conditions |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| PMC4618180.md | YES | PEG-IFN, The CC genotype is consistently the \"favorable\" genotype associated with better treatment response and spontaneous viral clearance.\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC2883666.md\n**Drug:** Pegylated significant independent predictor of response to PEG-IFN/RBV in patients with chronic HCV infection\"\n- \"rs12979860 CC predicted a sustained response with 78% specificity and 65% sensitivity in patients infected with HCV genotype 1\"\n- \"the rs12979860 'responder' allele of rs12979860 was statistically associated with a higher baseline viral load\"\n\n---\n\n### Spanish patients\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"rs12979860 may be a better predictor of response to the peg-IFN/RBV treatment among HCV/HIV-1 coinfected patients\" compared to rs8099917\n- rs12979860 C allele: OR = 2.8 (95% CI 1.8–4.6, p=9.9×10^-6) for SVR\n- \"four causal variants were in high linkage disequilibrium … with the tag rs12979860 variant (r²≥0.92)\"\n- \"homozygosity for the three of the four causal IL28B gene SNPs … was completely linked to the rs12979860 major CC genotype (r²=1.0)\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC3282030.md\n**Drug:** PEG-IFN + ribavirin (standard of care)\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV infection (genotype 1), Caucasian patients from Romania\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"SVR rate was directly related to the IL28B genotype: 73.1% in the C/C IL28B genotype vs. 43.7% in non-C/C patients (P = 0.0126)\"\n- Cites Lagging et al.: \"baseline IP-10 and a C genotype at rs12979860 independently predicted the first phase of viral decline and RVR\"\n- \"For the C/C genotype, the high SVR rates possibly justify treating these patients with double therapy (PegIFN + ribavirin) only, and for the C/T and T/T genotypes, triple therapy seems to be the best option\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC3653303.md\n**Drug:** Pegylated interferon + ribavirin\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV infection (genotype 1); gene expression profiling in liver biopsies\n\nKey excerpts:\n- CC vs. non-CC at rs12979860: 164 genes differentially expressed; CC individuals had \"significantly lower expression\" of ISGs including MX1, OAS1/2/3, IFIT1/2/3, ISG15 (2–4-fold lower)\n- \"miR-122 expression showed no correlation with rs12979860 genotype\"\n- \"the rs12979860 polymorphism is on a common haplotype block with a non-synonymous coding variant in exon 2 of IL28B, rs8103142\"\n- \"no significant differences were detected in IL28B mRNA expression levels in CC vs. non-CC individuals\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC3674704.md\n**Drug:** PEG-IFN-alpha + ribavirin (PEG-IFN-α/RBV)\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV infection (all genotypes) and spontaneous HCV clearance; Swiss Hepatitis C Cohort Study (540 Caucasians)\n\nKey excerpts:\n- A newly identified dinucleotide variant TT/-G (rs67272382+rs74597329) was compared to rs12979860\n- \"TT/-G still provided the strongest and most significant association … compared with rs12979860 (OR = 0.37 … P = 2.47^-5^)\"\n- SVR: rs12979860 CC=77%, CT=56%, TT=45%\n- \"IL28B mRNA expression is driven by the presence of one or two mutant alleles of TT/-G but not by rs12979860\"\n- \"Both rs12979860 and TT/-G are located within a CpG island\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC3779247.md\n**Drug:** PEG-IFN + ribavirin; study of dendritic cells and immune correlates of treatment response\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"rs12979860 showed a strong association with treatment response with ~60% of individuals with the most favorable genotype CC and only 25% of individuals with the least favorable genotype TT achieving SVR\"\n- \"rs12979860 proved to be a much stronger predictor compared to rs4803217 and ss469415590\" in this small cohort\n- \"SNP rs12979860 (CC>CT>TT) … strongly associated with treatment-induced as well as spontaneous clearance of HCV in HCV mono-infected as well as HIV-1/HCV co-infected individuals\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC4560372.md\n**Drug:** Pegylated interferon-alpha + ribavirin (PEG-IFN-α based regimen)\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV infection (genotype 1 and non-genotype 1), Thai patients; also CXCL10/DPP4 gene polymorphisms studied\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"IL28B rs12979860 CC genotype (OR = 3.12; 95% CI, 1.72–5.67; P < 0.001)\" independent predictor of SVR\n- \"rate of SVR was higher for the CC genotype … than for non-CC in both genotype 1 (60.6% vs. 29.4%, P < 0.001) and non-genotype 1 (69.4% vs. 49.1%, P < 0.05)\"\n- \"80.4% of Thai CHC patients had the favorable IL28B CC allele\"\n- \"CXCL10 rs56061981 non-GG genotype was associated with a higher rate of SVR … only in patients with HCV genotype 1 infection that had unfavorable IL28B rs12979860 CT or TT genotypes\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC4618180.md\n**Drug:** Pegylated interferon + ribavirin (PEG-IFN/RBV); IFNL4 genotype used as a treatment predictor\n**Condition:** Recently acquired HCV infection (acute < 6 months and early chronic 6–24 months); HIV-positive and HIV-negative cohorts\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"favourable IFNL4 genotype (rs12979860 CC versus non-CC)\" listed as a key hypothesis under investigation\n- IFNL4 genotype determined by sequencing of rs12979860 or rs8099917\n- rs12979860 CC vs non-CC used as a predictor of SVR in multivariable logistic regression\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC4689517.md\n**Drug:** Pegylated IFN-alpha-2b + ribavirin\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV infection, genotypes 2 and 3, Caucasian patients (large German registry, n > 1006)\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"Both preferable IFNL3 genotypes were associated with RVR (both p<0.0001) rather than with SVR (rs12979860: p=0.251)\"\n- \"99.2% of patients with IFNL3-(rs12979860)-CC were IFNL4-(ss469415590)-TT/TT\" — near-perfect correlation in Caucasians\n- \"CC-rs12979860 … higher RVR rates compared to the non-preferable ones (82% vs. 63%; p<0.0001)\"\n- In HCV genotype 2/3, rs12979860 predicts RVR but not SVR; RVR is the only independent predictor of SVR\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC4765184.md\n**Drug:** PEG-IFN-alpha + ribavirin; vitamin D pathway genes (VDR rs2228570, CYP2R1 rs10741657) also studied\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV genotype 4, Egyptian patients (n=200)\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"IL28B rs12979860 genes polymorphisms accurately assure SVR in naïve CHC G4 patients treated with low cost standard therapy\"\n- CC genotype: 61.5% SVR; CT: 31.5% SVR; TT: 12.5% SVR (Chi² p<0.001)\n- \"combining these two genes [VDR + CYP2R1] with IL28B rs12979860 C/C genotype increase the probability of SVR from 80% to reach 100%\"\n- \"combining VDR rs2228570 A/A genotype and IL28B C/T+T/T genotypes dramatically increase SVR from 29% to reach 80%\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC4892373.md\n**Drug:** Peginterferon-alpha + ribavirin (pegIFN–RBV)\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV/HIV co-infection, HCV genotypes 1, 3, and 4 (164 patients; Spanish cohort)\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"rs12979860 CC genotype was a strong predictor of SVR (3.7; 1.6–8.5; 0.002), independent of HCV genotype 3, serum HCV-RNA < 600,000 IU/ml, and lack of advanced liver fibrosis\"\n- \"SVR rate was higher in patients with CC than in those CT/TT genotypes: 56 of 75 (75%) versus 34 of 89 (38%) (P < 0.0001)\"\n- \"effect of the SNP was seen in HCV genotypes 1 and 4 but not in HCV genotype 3 carriers\"\n- \"frequency of the rs12979860 CC genotype in patients with spontaneous HCV clearance was significantly higher than in chronically HIV/HCV-coinfected patients (75 vs. 46%; P = 0.007)\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC4892378.md\n**Drug:** Peginterferon-alpha + ribavirin (pegIFNα–RBV)\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV/HIV co-infection, HCV genotypes 1, 3, 4 (196 patients; Spanish cohort)\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"IL28B CC genotype is a strong predictor of virological response … This effect is mediated by an increase in viral clearance during the first 12 weeks of treatment and is mainly seen in patients infected with HCV genotypes 1 and 4\"\n- \"CC patients showed significantly greater declines in serum HCV-RNA at weeks 4 and 12 (median decrease: 3.60 vs. 1.77 log IU/ml at week 4, P < 0.0001)\"\n- \"RVR only was seen in 17% of non-CC patients\" vs. 50% of CC patients\n- SVR: CC=76% vs. non-CC=36%\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC5301237.md\n**Drug:** Pegylated IFN-alpha + ribavirin (PEG IFN-α/RBV)\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV infection (genotype 1b primarily) and spontaneous HCV clearance; Chinese Han population\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"ss469415590, rs12979860, the level of baseline HCV RNA and platelet were as independent predictors for SVR\" in multivariate analysis\n- rs12979860 CC: SVR rate 73.5%; CT: 53.2%; TT: 39.5%\n- \"linkage disequilibrium … was only moderate\" between rs12979860 and ss469415590 in spontaneous resolvers (r²=0.62)\n- \"AUC was 0.64 for the set of ss469415590 and rs12979860\" together; elevated to 0.71 when combined with HCV RNA and platelet count\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC6863998.md\n**Drug:** PEG-IFN + ribavirin (PEG-IFN/RBV); mentions DAA (sofosbuvir + velpatasvir + voxilaprevir) relapse data\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV infection (genotype 1, 2, 3, 4), North Indian patients\n\nKey excerpts:\n- rs12979860 minor allele (T) frequency: 0.241 in CHC cases, 0.229 in healthy controls (not significantly different)\n- CC genotype strongly associated with RVR (OR for CC vs TT=0.08, p<0.0001) and SVR (OR=0.09, p=0.001) — meaning CC=favorable\n- \"C–T haplotype [CC for rs12979860 + TT for rs8099917] … OR = 4.28 for RVR (p<0.0001) and 3.81 for SVR (p=0.003)\"\n- \"relapse was observed to be less for CC genotype (1.2%) as compared to higher relapse (10.8%) for risk genotype CT and TT\" in DAA therapy context\n- rs12979860 and rs8099917 form a strong LD block in CHC patients (D′=0.98, r²=0.62)\n\n---\n\n## Summary of Drugs and Phenotypes\n\n**Drug consistently associated with rs12979860 across all papers:**\n- Pegylated interferon-alpha (PEG-IFN-alpha-2a or -2b) + ribavirin — the universal treatment studied\n- Protease inhibitors (boceprevir, telaprevir, faldaprevir) in triple therapy contexts (PMC4177494)\n- DAA combinations including sofosbuvir-based regimens, where rs12979860 still showed residual impact (PMC3991683, PMC6863998)\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions associated with rs12979860:**\n- Chronic hepatitis C (CHC), all genotypes (1, 2, 3, 4); effect strongest in genotype 1/4\n- HCV/HIV co-infection\n- Spontaneous HCV clearance (CC genotype favors clearance)\n- Chronic hepatitis B (HBV) — IFN-alpha response (noted in PMC3793390)\n- Liver fibrosis progression and hepatocellular carcinoma risk (noted in PMC6863998)\n- ISG (interferon-stimulated gene) expression levels in liver biopsies\n- HCV viral kinetics (first-phase viral decline, RVR, EVR)\n- Baseline HCV viral load (CC genotype paradoxically associated with higher baseline viral load)"},{"type":"text","text":"agentId: a28ec3d (for resuming to continue this agent's work if needed)\n<usage>total_tokens: 143762\ntool_uses: 24\nduration_ms: 138955</usage>"}]}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"17749463-2a4a-4a6b-86cf-6caa0d1fb526","uuid":"d4c982b6-cca7-4a13-9da3-54db3b9df729","tool_use_result":{"status":"completed","prompt":"Search all papers in /app/papers/ for mentions of rs12979860. The CC genotype is consistently the \"favorable\" genotype associated with better treatment response and spontaneous viral clearance.\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC2883666.md\n**Drug:** Pegylated interferon + ribavirin (PEG-IFN/RBV)\n**Condition:** Chronic hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection, genotypes 1, 2/3; Caucasian and African American patients\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"rs12979860 CC genotype (found in ~40% of Caucasians) predicted a sustained virologic response to therapy among Caucasians (odds ratio 5.79; 95% confidence interval 2.67–12.57; p=9.0 × 10^-6)\"\n- \"rs12979860 genotype is a significant independent predictor of response to PEG-IFN/RBV in patients with chronic HCV infection\"\n- \"rs12979860 CC predicted a sustained response with 78% specificity and 65% sensitivity in patients infected with HCV genotype 1\"\n- \"the rs12979860 'responder' allele of rs12979860 was statistically associated with a higher baseline viral load\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC3044738.md\n**Drug:** Pegylated interferon-alpha + ribavirin (peg-IFN/ribavirin)\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV infection, genotypes 1–4; Caucasian patients (HCV-DITTO trial)\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"baseline IP-10 and C genotype at rs12979860 independently predicted the first phase viral decline and RVR, which in turn independently predicted SVR\"\n- \"CC at rs12979860 … being associated with lower baseline IP-10 (P = 0.02)\" and \"higher baseline viral load\"\n- \"SVR was achieved in 85% … among homozygous carriers with baseline IP-10 below 150 pg/mL\" for rs12979860 CC\n- \"66% vs. 48% for rs12979860 CC and CT/TT, P = 0.04\" for SVR in genotype 1\n- rs12979860 and rs12980275 were in strong LD (R²=0.86)\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC3066089.md\n**Drug:** PEG-IFN-alpha-2a + ribavirin\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV/HIV co-infection (genotypes 1 and 3), South American patients\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"The CC genotype was overrepresented (p=0.015) in patients infected with HCV genotype-3 compared to genotype-1\"\n- \"In genotype-1 patients, the second-slower phase of viral decline (days 2–29) and infected-cells-loss rate were larger (p=0.02 and 0.11, respectively) in genotype-CC than in genotypes-TC/TT\"\n- \"first-phase-viral decline … were larger (trend P≤0.12) in genotype-CC compared with genotypes-TC/TT\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC3083026.md\n**Drug:** Pegylated IFN-alpha + ribavirin (PEG-IFN/RBV)\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV infection (genotype 1), Caucasian patients (VIRAHEP-C cohort, including African Americans)\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"Carriage of a C-allele at the IL28B gene polymorphism (rs12979860) is favorably associated with treatment response … SVR rates of 87% with CC, 50% with CT, and 39% with TT genotypes\"\n- \"IL28B genotype and baseline IP-10 levels are independent and additive predictors of HCV treatment response\"\n- \"Patients lacking IL28B C-allele(s) (rs12979860) in combination with high IP-10 levels may require alteration of therapy type and/or duration\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC3114195.md\n**Drug:** Pegylated IFN-alpha + ribavirin\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV infection (genotype 1), Taiwanese patients\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"rs12979860 is the only IL28B SNP showed significant association in multivariate analysis (P=2.3 × 10^-8, OR 0.177)\"\n- \"rs12979860 CC genotype and C allele are associated with spontaneous resolution of HCV infection\" in Caucasians and Africans, but no genotype/allele distribution difference was observed between HCV patients and normal controls in Taiwanese\n- \"eight SNPs with strong linkage disequilibrium demonstrate significant associations with SVR on single point analysis\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC3180021.md\n**Drug:** Pegylated interferon-alpha + ribavirin (PEG-IFN/RIB)\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV infection (genotype 1), Chilean patients (first Latin American analysis of these SNPs)\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"IL28B rs12979860 CC … genotypes were much more frequently found in patients with sustained virological response compared to null responders (38% … vs 2% … respectively). These differences were highly significant (P < 0.0001)\"\n- \"The CC genotype … the homozygous CC was found in 19 of 50 patients with SVR, vs 1 of 49 in NR patients\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC3273458.md\n**Drug:** Pegylated IFN-alpha + ribavirin (peg-IFN-α/RBV)\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV/HIV-1 co-infection; Spanish patients\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"rs12979860 may be a better predictor of response to the peg-IFN/RBV treatment among HCV/HIV-1 coinfected patients\" compared to rs8099917\n- rs12979860 C allele: OR = 2.8 (95% CI 1.8–4.6, p=9.9×10^-6) for SVR\n- \"four causal variants were in high linkage disequilibrium … with the tag rs12979860 variant (r²≥0.92)\"\n- \"homozygosity for the three of the four causal IL28B gene SNPs … was completely linked to the rs12979860 major CC genotype (r²=1.0)\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC3282030.md\n**Drug:** PEG-IFN + ribavirin (standard of care)\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV infection (genotype 1), Caucasian patients from Romania\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"SVR rate was directly related to the IL28B genotype: 73.1% in the C/C IL28B genotype vs. 43.7% in non-C/C patients (P = 0.0126)\"\n- Cites Lagging et al.: \"baseline IP-10 and a C genotype at rs12979860 independently predicted the first phase of viral decline and RVR\"\n- \"For the C/C genotype, the high SVR rates possibly justify treating these patients with double therapy (PegIFN + ribavirin) only, and for the C/T and T/T genotypes, triple therapy seems to be the best option\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC3653303.md\n**Drug:** Pegylated interferon + ribavirin\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV infection (genotype 1); gene expression profiling in liver biopsies\n\nKey excerpts:\n- CC vs. non-CC at rs12979860: 164 genes differentially expressed; CC individuals had \"significantly lower expression\" of ISGs including MX1, OAS1/2/3, IFIT1/2/3, ISG15 (2–4-fold lower)\n- \"miR-122 expression showed no correlation with rs12979860 genotype\"\n- \"the rs12979860 polymorphism is on a common haplotype block with a non-synonymous coding variant in exon 2 of IL28B, rs8103142\"\n- \"no significant differences were detected in IL28B mRNA expression levels in CC vs. non-CC individuals\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC3674704.md\n**Drug:** PEG-IFN-alpha + ribavirin (PEG-IFN-α/RBV)\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV infection (all genotypes) and spontaneous HCV clearance; Swiss Hepatitis C Cohort Study (540 Caucasians)\n\nKey excerpts:\n- A newly identified dinucleotide variant TT/-G (rs67272382+rs74597329) was compared to rs12979860\n- \"TT/-G still provided the strongest and most significant association … compared with rs12979860 (OR = 0.37 … P = 2.47^-5^)\"\n- SVR: rs12979860 CC=77%, CT=56%, TT=45%\n- \"IL28B mRNA expression is driven by the presence of one or two mutant alleles of TT/-G but not by rs12979860\"\n- \"Both rs12979860 and TT/-G are located within a CpG island\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC3779247.md\n**Drug:** PEG-IFN + ribavirin; also mentions vitamin D receptor (VDR) gene polymorphisms\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV infection (genotype 1), Caucasian patients; also interaction with vitamin D pathway\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"IL28B rs12979860 T allele carrier (no/yes): Odds ratio 6.82 (3.75–12.41)\" for non-SVR; adjusted OR=8.724 (p<0.001)\n- CC genotype: 54/79 SVR (68%); T-allele carriers: 9/120 SVR (7.5%)\n- \"Complementary role of vitamin D deficiency and the interleukin-28B rs12979860 C/T polymorphism in predicting antiviral response in chronic hepatitis C\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC3793390.md\n**Drug:** Pegylated interferon-alpha + ribavirin (pegIFN-α/RBV)\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV infection (CHC) and spontaneous HCV clearance; African-American and European-American populations; discovery of IFNL4 gene\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"ss469415590-ΔG allele is perfectly correlated with the unfavorable rs12979860-T allele in Asians (r²=1.00) and well correlated in Europeans (r²=0.92)\" but only moderate in Africans (r²=0.71)\n- \"among African-American individuals, ss469415590 is a better marker than rs12979860 for predicting response to pegIFN-α/RBV treatment\"\n- \"rs12979860 predicts early viral kinetics in HCV-infected patients receiving IFN-α-free treatment\"\n- \"rs12979860 has also been associated with response to IFN-α based treatment of chronic hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection\"\n- \"IFNL4 induces weak expression of ISGs … but also reduces the responsiveness to type-I and type-III IFNs\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC3991683.md\n**Drug:** Pegylated interferon + ribavirin (pegIFN/RBV)\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV genotype 1 or 4, Caucasian patients (mostly HIV/HCV co-infected)\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"For rs12979860, 66 (64.0%) CC versus 56 (33.1%) T allele carriers achieved SVR (Adjusted OR = 4.156, 95%CI = 2.388–7.232, p = 4.647×10^-7)\"\n- \"ss469415590 shows an equivalent performance to predict SVR to pegIFN/RBV than the rs12979860 variant in Caucasian HCV-1/4-infected patients\"\n- \"rs12979860 genotype also impacts on the outcome of most DAA-based regimens including pegIFN\" and \"has a role in the viral kinetics and response in interferon-free regimens\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC4177494.md\n**Drug:** PEG-IFN-alpha-2a or -2b + ribavirin; study of dendritic cells and immune correlates of treatment response\n\nKey excerpts:\n- \"rs12979860 showed a strong association with treatment response with ~60% of individuals with the most favorable genotype CC and only 25% of individuals with the least favorable genotype TT achieving SVR\"\n- \"rs12979860 proved to be a much stronger predictor compared to rs4803217 and ss469415590\" in this small cohort\n- \"SNP rs12979860 (CC>CT>TT) … strongly associated with treatment-induced as well as spontaneous clearance of HCV in HCV mono-infected as well as HIV-1/HCV co-infected individuals\"\n\n---\n\n### /app/papers/PMC4560372.md\n**Drug:** Pegylated interferon-alpha + ribavirin (PEG-IFN-α based regimen)\n**Condition:** Chronic HCV infection (genotype 1 and non-genotype 1), Thai patients; Camma C, Petta S, Enea M, et al. Cost-effectiveness of boceprevir or telaprevir for untreated patients with genotype 1 chronic hepatitis C. Hepatology (Baltimore, Md) 2012;56(3):850–60. doi: 10.1002/hep.25734.  [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1002/hep.25734) | [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22454336/) | [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?journal=Hepatology%20(Baltimore,%20Md)&title=Cost-effectiveness%20of%20boceprevir%20or%20telaprevir%20for%20untreated%20patients%20with%20genotype%201%20chronic%20hepatitis%20C&author=C%20Camma&author=S%20Petta&author=M%20Enea&volume=56&issue=3&publication_year=2012&pages=850-60&pmid=22454336&doi=10.1002/hep.25734&)\n6. Zeuzem S, Andreone P, Pol S, Lawitz E, Diago M, Roberts S, Focaccia R, Younossi Z, Foster GR, Horban A, et al. Telaprevir for retreatment of HCV infection. N Engl J Med. 2011;364:2417–2428. doi: 10.1056/NEJMoa1013086.  [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1013086) | [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21696308/) | [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?journal=N%20Engl%20J%20Med&title=Telaprevir%20for%20retreatment%20of%20HCV%20infection&author=S%20Zeuzem&author=P%20Andreone&author=S%20Pol&author=E%20Lawitz&author=M%20Diago&volume=364&publication_year=2011&pages=2417-2428&pmid=21696308&doi=10.1056/NEJMoa1013086&)\n8. Gellad ZF, Reed SD, Muir AJ. Economic evaluation of direct-acting antiviral therapy in chronic hepatitis C. Antivir Ther. 2012;17:1189–1199. doi: 10.3851/IMP2430.  [DOI](https://doi.org/10.3851/IMP2430) | [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23186646/) | [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?journal=Antivir%20Ther&title=Economic%20evaluation%20of%20direct-acting%20antiviral%20therapy%20in%20chronic%20hepatitis%20C&author=ZF%20Gellad&author=SD%20Reed&author=AJ%20Muir&volume=17&publication_year=2012&pages=1189-1199&pmid=23186646&doi=10.3851/IMP2430&)\nAlthough *IFNL4* ss469415590 is strongly associated with HCV clearance, it is uncertain that these genetic markers will be still needed if the upcoming IFN-free therapy will become available. We think this conjecture is not well founded. Firstly, many new treatments such as Daclatasvir and Sofosbuvir were still in clinical trials and such high payment lead it will not be available for the foreseeable future in developing countries[21](#b21). Secondly, with significantly high SVR rates and safety, these IFN-free therapies were researched only in recent years and still needed a long follow-up time to assess the safety and efficacy[22](#b22). Thus, interferon-based therapies will be used for many years in most countries. Finally, in the recent paper on the triple therapy with pegylated IFN-α2b, RBV and telaprevir, 176 patients with *IFNL4* ss469415590 TT/TT had better response rates than those carrying TT/ΔG or ΔG/ΔG alleles, suggesting genetics may still play a role for new therapy[16](#b16).\nBecause of significant decline in HIV-related morbidity since the introduction of HAART, liver disease caused by chronic HCV infection has emerged as a major problem in HIV-1/HCV co-infected individuals. HIV co-infection has a negative impact on HCV pathogenesis, and therefore it is recommended to control HIV-1 levels prior to beginning HCV treatment in co-infected individuals. It is also important to note that despite successful response to HAART, many individuals fail to achieve full functional restoration of their immune system. If such individuals get infected with HCV, their ability to mount a successful immune response against HCV could be compromised. HIV-1/HCV co-infection should thus be considered a pathological state with both unique and common features of HIV-1 and HCV mono-infection. Although our immune response can clear HCV [[37](#R37)], virus exposure often proceeds to chronicity. PEG-IFN and RBV combination therapy has been the mainstay of HCV treatment. IFN is a potent antiviral cytokine but is very unpleasant for the patient because of significant side effects such as depression, anemia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia, diarrhea, and flulike symptoms such as fever, chills, fatigue, headache, and muscle aches. Besides, in individuals infected with the difficult-to-treat HCV genotypes (genotypes 1 and 4), it is curative only 40% 50% of the time. Although first-generation direct-acting antiviral drugs are now being introduced into the clinic; high cost and emergence of drug resistant HCV variants remain important concerns. IFN/RBV will thus be an essential part of HCV treatment and therefore a thorough understanding of its mechanism of action is necessary in order to identify host factors that predictive IFN/RBV treatment response.\nChronic C hepatitis is a global healthcare problem, affecting approximately 3% of the world population [[1](#R1)], and is an endemic disease in some areas [[2](#R2)]. The number of patients requiring treatment for this disease is increasing; therefore, the treatment costs, at the global level, are huge. Reference studies report sustained virologic response (SVR) rates ranging from 34% to 61% in patients infected with genotype 1 HCV, in connection with ethnicity, gender, age, severity of fibrosis, or viral load [[3](#R3)][[4](#R4)][[5](#R5)]. Multiple factors influencing SVR rates have been evaluated, to find the best candidates to achieve viral clearance. Recently, several papers reported that a genetic polymorphism near the IL28B gene, encoding interferon-lambda-3, is associated with an approximately 2-fold change in treatment response [[6](#R6)][[7](#R7)][[8](#R8)]. Several previous studies showed that SVR rates vary in patients according to their ethnicity, with lower rates in the Afro-American population followed by Caucasians, and with the best results occurring in Asian populations. These results can be possibly explained by genetic polymorphisms in the IL28B gene in different populations. In Romania, 99.7% of HCV patients are infected with genotype 1, the most difficult to treat [[9](#R9)]. A recently published epidemiological study showed that 3.23% of the Romanian population is chronically infected with HCV [[10](#R10)]. Thus, considerable financial effort is needed to treat this large number of patients; therefore, if very good predictors for SVR are found, different approaches should be used for these patients. New drugs for the treatment of chronic hepatitis C (telaprevir or boceprevir) are expected on the market in the next year, with better therapeutic results (compared to pegylated interferon [PegIFN] and ribavirin alone), but probably with high financial impact. New therapeutic strategies for the stratification of patients may therefore be needed in order to increase SVR rates with minimal cost increase.\nChronic infections with human immunodeficiency virus 1 (HIV-1) and hepatitis C virus (HCV) are a leading cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide. Chronic infection with HIV-1 causes acquired immunodeficiency syndrome [[1](#R1)], which is characterized by severe immunosuppression and associated opportunistic infections, whereas chronic HCV infection causes chronic liver disease and its associated complications such as liver cirrhosis, liver failure and hepatocellular carcinoma [[2](#R2)]. HCV genotype 1 is the most prevalent and difficult to treat genotype [[3](#R3)]. Up to 30% of HIV-infected individuals overall and 60–90% of HIV-infected injection drug users are infected with HCV [[4](#R4)–[6](#R6)] and therefore HIV-1/HCV co-infection is a significant health problem. Since co-infection with HIV-1 is known to have a negative impact on HCV pathogenesis and disease progression, it is recommended that in individuals with HIV-1/HCV co-infection, HIV-1 be controlled prior to initiation of HCV treatment [[7](#R7)]. PEG-Interferon-α (PEG-IFN)/Ribavirin (RBV) combination therapy has been the mainstay of chronic HCV treatment but unlike HAART it has been difficult because of limited success rate (~50%) and toxic side effects. Both PEG-IFN and RBV have nonspecific and largely unknown mechanisms of action and therefore in the last few years direct-acting antiviral agents (DAAs) have been developed and approved for treating HCV. Administration of DAAs with/without IFN/RBV has improved cure rates but the high cost of DAAs along with emergence of drug-resistant HCV variants remain important concerns. Therefore, for better clinical management of HCV, it is necessary to identify host factors that predict the outcome of IFN/RBV treatment. Individuals with undetectable HCV RNA levels six months or longer after IFN/RBV treatment are referred to as sustained virological responders (SVRs) and those that fail to clear HCV after treatment are referred to as non-responders (NRs). As sentinels of our immune system, DCs play a central role in initiating and regulating protective antiviral immunity. Immature DCs express inflammatory chemokine receptor CCR5 whose interaction with chemokine CCL5 promotes their recruitment to the site of infection. At the site of infection, DCs sense viral signatures through pattern recognition receptors (PRRs) and become activated. Upon activation, DCs undergo a process of maturation which involves increase in the surface expression of major histocompatibility complex (MHC) class I and II molecules, co-stimulatory molecules (such as CD86), adhesion molecules (such as CD54 and CD62L) and lymph node homing receptors (such as CCR7) [[8](#R8)]. The cellular immune response has a profound impact on outcome and pathogenesis of HCV infection [[9](#R9)]. Poor CD4^+^ and CD8^+^ T cell response is a hallmark of chronic HCV infection [[10](#R10)]. Being professional antigen presenting cells, DCs play a crucial role in directing an efficient antiviral T-cell response. Functional defects in DCs have been shown to underlie weak and narrowly focused T cell response characteristic of chronic HCV infection [[11](#R11),[12](#R12)]. Compared to uninfected individuals, DCs isolated from HCV-infected individuals are less efficient in stimulating T cell activation and proliferation [[12](#R12)–[14](#R14)]. HCV-infected individuals have increased expression of programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) on DCs [[15](#R15),[16](#R16)]. Binding of PD-L1 to programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-1) expressed on T cells delivers a coinhibitory signal to T cells resulting in their reduced proliferation and effector functions. Also, HIV-1-infected [[17](#R17)–[21](#R21)] and HCV-infected [[12](#R12),[22](#R22)–[24](#R24)] individuals have reduced frequencies of mDCs and pDCs compared to uninfected individuals. Since IFN/RBV therapy is known to enhance HCV-specific CD4 and CD8 T-cell response in SVRs, it is possible that IFN/RBV therapy restores both DC frequencies and functions in SVRs but not in NRs. Most studies that have investigated the role of DCs in IFN/RBV treatment outcome in HCV mono-infected or HIV-1/HCV co-infected individuals have mostly focused on HCV genotype 1 since it is the most prevalent and difficult to treat HCV genotype. Herein, we investigated a cohort of HIV-1/HCV (HCV genotype 1) co-infected individuals to study whether IFN/RBV treatment differentially affects DC frequencies and phenotype in NRs and SVRs. We also genotyped three important SNPs associated with treatment response. Collectively, this study helped identify the genetic and immune correlates of IFN/RBV treatment response in HIV-1/HCV individuals.\nDespite moving to the new era of CHC treatment with direct-acting antiviral agents (DAA), in most developing countries, including Thailand, where DAAs are not widely accessible, the PEG-IFN-α based treatment remains an effective therapy for CHC infection. Several factors are potential predictors for treatment response with PEG-IFN-α based regimen and may improve predictive efficiency of *IL-28B* genotype for treatment outcome in CHC-infected patients, including baseline γ-glutamyltransferase/alanine aminotransferase ratio, vitamin D level, serum ferritin concentration, and early anemia. However, available data are contradictory [[30](#pone.0137365.ref030)–[35](#pone.0137365.ref035)]. In this current study, we did not collect baseline γ-glutamyltransferase, serum vitamin D and ferritin. Additionally, we intended to study the association of pre-treatment factors and SVR, thus we did not include RVR or EVR in multivariate analysis for SVR. However, we found in univariate analysis that RVR and EVR during therapy predicted SVR.\nFor years, the standard of care for hepatitis C virus genotype 1 (HCV-G1) infected patients consisted of 48 wk of combination therapy with pegylated-interferon α and ribavirin (PEG-IFN/RBV)[[1](#B1)]. This treatment regimen underwent a major change after the introduction of direct antiviral agents (DAAs). DAAs directly inhibit specific steps in the HCV life cycle, for example, the two currently approved molecules, boceprevir and telaprevir, target NS3/4A serine protease (protease inhibitors, PIs)[[2](#B2)]. Triple therapy with PIs improves on-treatment kinetics and increases sustained virological response (SVR) rate with decreased duration of therapy in both naïve and treatment-experienced HCV-G1 patients[[3](#B3)-[6](#B6)], but raises concerns regarding the generation of resistant viral variants and significant side effects[[7](#B7)]. Furthermore, PIs are expensive and are not yet available in many countries[[8](#B8)].\nHIV-1/HCV co-infection is a significant health problem. Highly active antiretroviral treatment (HAART) against HIV-1 has proved to be fairly successful. On the other hand, direct acting antiviral drugs against HCV have improved cure rates but high cost and development of drug resistance are important concerns. Therefore PEGylated interferon (PEG-IFN) and ribavirin (RBV) still remain essential components of HCV treatment, and identification of host factors that predict IFN/RBV treatment response is necessary for effective clinical management of HCV infection. Impaired dendritic cell (DC) and T cell responses are associated with HCV persistence. It has been shown that IFN/RBV treatment enhances HCV-specific T cell functions and it is likely that functional restoration of DCs is the underlying cause. To test this hypothesis, we utilized an antibody cocktail (consisting of DC maturation, adhesion and other surface markers) to perform comprehensive phenotypic characterization of myeloid DCs (mDCs) and plasmacytoid DCs (pDCs) in a cohort of HIV-1/HCV co-infected individuals undergoing IFN/RBV treatment. Our results show that pre-treatment frequencies of mDCs are lower in non-responders (NRs) compared to responders (SVRs) and healthy controls. Although, the treatment was able to restore the frequency of mDCs in NRs, it downregulated the frequency of CCR7+, CD54+ and CD62L+ mDCs. Pre-treatment frequencies of pDCs were lower in NRs and decreased further upon treatment. Compared to SVRs, NRs exhibited higher ratio of PD-L1+/CD86+ pDCs prior to treatment; and this ratio remained high even after treatment. These findings demonstrate that enumeration and phenotypic assessment of DCs before/during therapy can help predict the treatment outcome. We also show that before treatment, PBMCs from SVRs secrete higher amounts of IFN-γ compared to controls and NRs. Upon genotyping IFNL3 polymorphisms rs12979860, rs4803217 and ss469415590, we found rs12979860 to be a better predictor of treatment outcome. Collectively, our study led to identification of important correlates of IFN/RBV treatment response in HIV-1/HCV co-infected individuals.\nIFNL3 genotyping from serum was highly efficient and can be used as an alternative if EDTA whole blood is not available. In Caucasian GT2/3 patients genotyping for INFL4-(ss469415590) does not lead to additional information for the decision-making process. Importantly, IFNL3 SNPs were not associated with SVR but with RVR. Even in the era of new direct acting antiviral (DAA) therapies, IFNL3 testing may therefore still be considered for naïve GT2/3 patients to decide if dual Peg-IFN/RBV therapy is an option in resource limited regions.\nIn the era of DAAs, *IL28B* testing offers the minimal additional information that could influence the clinician’s decision[[27](#B27)]. Nevertheless, triple therapy is not recommended in all patients and the evaluation of pre-treatment factors such as the *IL28B* SNPs still holds significance to establish the therapeutic schedule. Moreover, in a recent review, Matsuura et al[[30](#B30)] reported that *IL28B* polymorphisms may affect viral kinetics even in the context of IFN-free regimens. In a phase 2, randomized, open-label trial of faldaprevir (NS3/4A protease inhibitor) and deleobuvir (NS5B polymerase inhibitor), the SVR rates tended to be higher in patients with CC at rs12979860 than in those with non-CC. This suggests that innate immunity may still be important and confirms the importance of *IL28B* genotyping in the context of new and future therapy regimens[[30](#B30)].\nInfection with hepatitis C virus (HCV) progresses to chronic infection in 75% of cases, often leading to cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma ([1](#R1)). SNPs rs12979860 and ss469415590 are located upstream of IFNL3 whereas SNP rs4803217 is harbored in the 3′ UTR of IFNL3. (B) Table showing the race, sex, treatment response as well as rs12979860, rs4803217 and ss469415590 genotypes (determined using custom designed TaqMan qPCR based allelic discrimination assay, Life Technologies) in a cohort of HIV-1/HCV co-infected individuals utilized in the study. Clearance rate was higher in patients carrying the beneficial TT/TT genotype (39.4%) than in patients with TT/ΔG (23.8%) or ΔG/ΔG (18.7%) (*P* < 0.001). Meanwhile, *IL28B* rs12979860 variants were also negatively related with viral clearance and clearance rate was 38.9%, 27.6% and 17.2% for CC, CT and TT genotype (*P* < 0.001). After adjusting for age, gender, and viral genotype, logistic regression analyses showed that *IFNL4* ss469415590 and *IL28B* rs12979860 variant genotypes significantly decreased the ability of host HCV clearance (dominant model: OR = 0.50, 95% CI = 0.36–0.71 for ss469415590; OR = 0.59, 95% CI = 0.42–0.83 for rs12979860) ([Table 2](#t2)). This effect of ss469415590 and rs12979860 on HCV clearance remained in existence after being conditioned on each other (*P* = 0.005 for ss469415590 and *P* = 0.016 for rs12979860) and therefore they was included in further combined analyses by adding up the number of unfavorable alleles of the independent SNPs ss469415590-ΔG and rs12979860-T. The result showed that subjects carrying three to four unfavorable alleles had a 90% decrease in clearance in comparison with those without unfavorable allele (OR = 0.10, 95% CI = 0.02–0.42) ([Table 3](#t3)).\n*VDR* gene polymorphisms are independently related to the response to Peg-IFN+RBV therapy in chronic hepatitis C and could be used as complementary biomarkers of response when included in a prediction algorithm in association with demographic, virologic, biochemical and genetic traits.\n1.
